did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid AI model for skin cancer detection from smartphone images. The method involves preprocessing with adaptive thresholding and black-hat transformation, feature extraction using a Vision Transformer (ViT), and classification using a stacking model that combines ViT features with clinical variables via XGBoost. The study evaluates this model on six categories of skin lesions (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, nevus, squamous cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It details a proposed hybrid AI model (using ViT, adaptive thresholding, black-hat transformation, and XGBoost) for skin cancer detection, describes the methodology including data preprocessing and model architecture, reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 97.61% accuracy) on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, and includes sections for Materials and Methods, Results, and Discussion.

title: AI-driven skin cancer detection from smartphone images: A hybrid model using ViT, adaptive thresholding, black-hat transformation, and XGBoost
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid AI model for detecting skin cancer using smartphone images and clinical metadata. The approach preprocesses images with adaptive thresholding and Black-Hat transformation, extracts features via a Vision Transformer (ViT), and classifies lesions using a stacking model that combines ViT features with patient data via XGBoost. The model was evaluated on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, which contains 2,298 images across six skin lesion categories including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The method achieved an overall accuracy of 97.61%, a positive predictive value of 96.88%, and a recall of 97.63%. These results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed hybrid model in accurately identifying malignant skin lesions. The authors conclude that this smartphone-based system could enhance diagnostic sensitivity and facilitate timely patient care.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the six categories in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset used for classification. The model achieved a PPV of 0.972028 and recall of 0.952055. Misclassifications occurred with SCC and SEK.
One of the six categories in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. Classified as a high-risk category with 100% PPV, recall, F1-score, and NPV. 169 correct predictions reported.
One of the six categories in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. Classified as a high-risk category with 100% PPV, recall, F1-score, and NPV. 10 correct predictions reported.
One of the six categories in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. Classified as a high-risk category with 100% PPV, recall, F1-score, and NPV. 49 correct predictions reported.

One of the six categories in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The model encountered difficulties classifying this malignant lesion, with a PPV of 0.840909 and recall of 0.948718. Challenges in differentiating from ACK noted.

all_task_types.target: Six discrete categories of skin lesions: actinic keratosis (ACK), basal cell carcinoma (BCC), melanoma (MEL), nevus (NEV), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and seborrheic keratosis (SEK).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a 'classification' task. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using a stacking model that combines Vision Transformer (ViT) features with clinical variables via XGBoost. The target variable is encoded numerically (0-5) for the six lesion classes. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy (97.61%), positive predictive value (96.88%), recall (97.63%), and F1-score (97.19%).

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising smartphone images of skin lesions and associated clinical metadata collected by the Dermatological and Surgical Assistance Program (PAD) at the Federal University of Espírito Santo (UFES) in Brazil during 2018 and 2019. The dataset includes 2,298 images in PNG format and 26 clinical features per lesion in a CSV file. Images were captured using various smartphones, leading to variable resolutions. The dataset is publicly available via Mendeley Data.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid model (ViT feature extraction and XGBoost classification) after preprocessing (adaptive thresholding, Black-Hat transformation, resizing to 224x224, and clinical feature selection).
use.validation: Used for validation of the model performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance.
use.other: The dataset was partitioned into training, testing, and validation subsets, but specific counts for these splits were not reported in the text.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To improve generalization and provide robust and unbiased performance estimates, especially with limited and imbalanced data.
To address class imbalance and enhance model learning by ensuring minority classes had a stronger impact during training.
To prioritize hard-to-classify examples and handle imbalanced datasets during training.
To automatically calculate class weights for handling class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, positive predictive value (PPV), recall, and F1-score.

actinic_keratosis.notes: Misclassifications occurred with SCC and SEK.

squamous_cell_carcinoma.notes: Challenges in differentiating from ACK noted due to morphological similarities.

comparison_with_state_of_the_art.note: The proposed model surpassed all evaluated models in PPV, recall, and F1 score. Rival models' PPV varied from 70.7% to 92.1%.

baseline_models.dataset: RGB ISIC dataset of dermoscopic Images
HSI ISIC dataset of dermoscopic Images

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid model combining Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) for feature extraction and XGBoost for classification via stacking

architecture_details.input_variants: Three variants processed in parallel: Original image, Black-Hat transformed image, and Adaptive thresholded image
architecture_details.feature_fusion: Element-wise multiplication of features from original and transformed images, followed by concatenation along the x-axis
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected layer producing probability scores, fed into a stacking model with clinical metadata (age, sex) via XGBoost, with final logistic regression output

training_details.data_split: 80% training (1838 images), 20% testing (460 images), 20% of training set for validation
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i5-10300H CPU, 16GB RAM, NVIDIA GeForce RTX3060 GPU

classification_or_output_head.type: Stacking model with XGBoost and Logistic Regression
classification_or_output_head.inputs: ViT deep features concatenated with clinical metadata (patient age and sex)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 224x224x3 pixels to maintain data integrity and optimal model performance
Black-Hat transformation (morphological closing followed by subtraction from original image) to highlight dark regions and pigmentation
Adaptive Gaussian thresholding to segment images based on local brightness fluctuations
Grayscale conversion for morphological operations and thresholding
Gaussian blur for noise mitigation and improvement of image quality
Element-wise multiplication of feature sets from original, Black-Hat, and adaptive thresholding images
Feature concatenation along the x-axis to create a unified representation
Class encoding of lesion labels (ACK=0, BCC=1, MEL=2, NEV=3, SCC=4, SEK=5)
Omission of non-predictive metadata features (patient_id, lesion_id, image_id, diagnosis, area) to prevent overfitting
Handling of missing values in clinical metadata (approx. 35% missing rate for features like smoke, drink, age, gender, etc.)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Morphological operation used to highlight dark regions and pigmentation. Process involves converting image to grayscale, defining a kernel to match dark features, performing morphological closing (dilation followed by erosion), and subtracting the result from the original image. Used as a feature enhancement strategy rather than traditional augmentation.

Technique used to segment images based on local brightness fluctuations. Threshold for each pixel is determined within a localized window using a Gaussian distribution and a constant C. Formula: T(x, y) = (1/n) * sum(I(i,j) * G(i,j)) - C. Used to emphasize characteristics in areas of interest concealed by global thresholding.

The paper explicitly states it does not utilize traditional data augmentation techniques like rotation, flipping, or scaling. Instead, it employs a strategy centered on feature enhancement by producing two supplementary representations (IBlack Hat and IAdaptive) in addition to the original image (I). These three images serve as simultaneous inputs to the Vision Transformer to enhance sensitivity to clinically significant yet infrequent lesion types.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid AI model, combining Vision Transformer (ViT) feature extraction with XGBoost classification using clinical metadata, achieved an overall accuracy of 97.61%, a positive predictive value of 96.88%, a recall of 97.63%, and an F1 score of 97.19% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

The model outperformed all evaluated state-of-the-art architectures, many of which utilized clinical-grade dermoscopic images, demonstrating the efficacy of smartphone images for skin cancer detection when combined with appropriate preprocessing and clinical variables.

The method effectively identifies malignant skin lesions across six categories (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, nevus, squamous cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis), with perfect performance (100% PPV, recall, F1-score, and NPV) reported for basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, and nevus classes.

The study acknowledges challenges in differentiating squamous cell carcinoma from actinic keratosis due to morphological similarities, resulting in lower performance metrics for SCC compared to other classes.
The integration of interpretability tools (Grad-CAM and SHAP) enhances the transparency and trustworthiness of the model by visualizing image-based attention and clinical feature importance.
The authors conclude that this smartphone-based system could enhance diagnostic sensitivity and specificity, reduce delays in identifying suspicious lesions, and facilitate timely patient care.
Future work will focus on incorporating additional data types, such as histopathology images and genetic markers, and evaluating the model's generalizability across various clinical environments.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset (2,298 images) without external validation on independent datasets, limiting generalizability assessment.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, which was addressed via class weighting and Focal Loss, but the small sample size for high-risk classes like Melanoma (10 correct predictions) raises concerns about robustness.
The model struggles to differentiate Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) from Actinic Keratosis (ACK) due to morphological similarities, resulting in a lower PPV (84.09%) for SCC compared to other classes.
Images were captured using various smartphones, introducing heterogeneity in lighting conditions, image quality, and skin tones, which may affect performance in diverse real-world settings.
The paper acknowledges the absence of publicly accessible datasets with similar characteristics (smartphone images with clinical metadata) for future validation.
The conclusion section contains a contradiction, stating features were derived with 'Mobile Net' while the abstract and methods describe the use of a Vision Transformer (ViT).
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics only.
The study did not use traditional data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, scaling) but instead relied on feature enhancement via Black-Hat transformation and adaptive thresholding.
